headline: No Topic. Utterly Predictable.
subtext: One might assume a *task* requires a *subject*. Apparently, that's optional now.
body:
**No Topic. Utterly Predictable.**

Residents of Little Piddlington-on-Thames were once again subjected to the peculiar ritual of 'Agenda Item 4.C.' at Tuesday’s Parish Council meeting. Officially designated "Miscellaneous Matters Arising From Previously Undocumented Concerns," the item is colloquially known as "The One Where Nothing Happens, But Very Loudly."

For three and a half hours, council members meticulously debated the existential implications of a newly painted white line outside Mrs. Higginbotham’s house. Councillor Derek Pringle questioned its "semiotic integrity," while Councillor Brenda Flummery demanded photographic evidence of its "pre-painted non-existence." The discussion then veered sharply into the optimal societal benefit of gravel versus bark chippings in community play areas, punctuated by a resident's impassioned plea about his neighbour’s sentient garden gnome collection.

"We have a mandate to address the minutiae," declared Council Leader Bertram Squeak, his voice barely audible over the whirring of the 1970s-era overhead projector displaying a blurry image of a pigeon. "Without this rigorous engagement with... well, *this*, where would we be? Utter chaos, I tell you! Anarchy of the garden statuary!"

Ultimately, a motion was passed to form a sub-committee to investigate the historical context of white lines, reporting back in approximately fiscal year 2027. The pigeon slide was then voted "Most Ambitious Use of Transparency Film," bringing the meeting to a close.
